figures.gloss: The individual ritual specialist of the Anglo-Caribbean, hired privately to heal, protect, divine, win court cases, or harm — working with herbs, grave dirt, and spirits of the dead rather than leading any congregation. From 1760 onward colonial statutes made the role itself a crime, so the practitioner worked, and often still works, under legal shadow.
figures.facet: A religious specialist defined by the client relationship instead of the congregation: obeah has no church, no communal liturgy, and no supreme cult — only the contracted transaction between practitioner, spirit, and paying client.
figures.provenance: Diana Paton, 'The Cultural Politics of Obeah' (2015); Jerome Handler & Kenneth Bilby, 'Enacting Power: The Criminalization of Obeah in the Anglophone Caribbean, 1760-2011' (2012). The word's derivation is contested: Twi 'obayifo'/'bayi' (witch/witchcraft) versus Igbo 'dibia'/'obia' (healer, esoteric knowledge) — Handler & Bilby (2001) argue for the Igbo-area root; unresolved.

figures.gloss: A worked assemblage of grave dirt, blood, feathers, eggshells, bones, rum, and pins — buried at a doorstep, hung in a tree, or carried on the body — that houses and directs spiritual force toward protection or attack. Colonial court records preserve inventories of confiscated bundles in obsessive detail.
figures.facet: The clearest material signature of the craft: obeah's power is archived not in scripture but in constructed objects, which is precisely why colonial law prosecuted possession of the objects themselves.
figures.provenance: Jerome Handler & Kenneth Bilby, 'Enacting Power' (2012); Diana Paton, 'The Cultural Politics of Obeah' (2015) — both draw on trial records itemizing seized obeah paraphernalia.

figures.gloss: The paired techniques of capturing a restless ghost — often bottled or pinned with rituals at the grave — and 'setting' it upon a client's enemy, or conversely pulling a set duppy off an afflicted victim. The obeah specialist is the recognized professional for both directions of the traffic.
figures.facet: The dead are treated as a deployable workforce: the same specialist who weaponizes a ghost is the one hired to disarm it, making obeah simultaneously the disease and the cure in the local spirit economy.
figures.provenance: Martha Warren Beckwith, 'Black Roadways: A Study of Jamaican Folk Life' (1929); Diana Paton, 'The Cultural Politics of Obeah' (2015).

figures.gloss: The binding loyalty oath administered by obeah specialists to conspirators in slave uprisings — most famously Tacky's Revolt (Jamaica, 1760) — sworn while drinking a mixture of rum, grave dirt, and blood. Its power to hold rebel networks silent is what drove the first Obeah Act into law that same year.
figures.facet: Obeah at its most political: a spoken-and-swallowed formula strong enough that the colonial state answered it with capital legislation, criminalizing a form of words.
figures.provenance: Vincent Brown, 'Tacky's Revolt: The Story of an Atlantic Slave War' (2020); Edward Long, 'The History of Jamaica' (1774), the hostile primary account of the oath and the 1760 Act.

figures.gloss: A Jamaican outlaw who led a small band in the Blue Mountains in 1780–81, reputedly carrying an obeah charm that made him invulnerable; hunted down and killed by Maroon fighters (tradition credits a Maroon named Quashee), who took his severed fingers as trophy and gave him his nickname.
figures.facet: The bandit's obeah charm, not his robberies, is what fixed him in the colonial imagination: within twenty years his story had become a stage franchise (William Earle's 1800 novel, John Fawcett's 1800 Haymarket pantomime 'Obi; or, Three-Finger'd Jack') that shipped a fictionalized obeah-armed rebel across the Atlantic stage circuit long before ethnographers ever described the actual craft.
figures.provenance: Benjamin Moseley, 'A Treatise on Sugar' (1799), an early printed account linking Jack to obeah; Diana Paton, 'The Cultural Politics of Obeah' (2015); Srinivas Aravamudan's edition and introduction to William Earle's 'Obi; or, The History of Three-Fingered Jack' (Broadview, 2005).

figures.gloss: An early-to-mid-18th-century leader of the Jamaican Windward Maroons, credited in Maroon oral tradition and colonial record alike with formidable spiritual powers — including catching enemy bullets — and named a Jamaican National Hero in 1976. That her powers were specifically 'obeah' is chiefly a hostile colonial framing rather than a Maroon self-description; Maroon tradition speaks of her authority in its own terms of ancestral and spiritual leadership, not necessarily the obeah idiom of the hired specialist.
figures.facet: The roster's clearest case of attribution creep: obeah's most famous woman is famous for a war leadership colonial writers chose to label obeah, which is itself a data point about how the term was weaponized to criminalize any Black spiritual authority the colonial state could not otherwise categorize.
figures.provenance: Jamaican National Heritage Trust designation (1976); Karla Gottlieb, 'The Mother of Us All: A History of Queen Nanny, Leader of the Windward Jamaican Maroons' (2000); Diana Paton, 'The Cultural Politics of Obeah' (2015), on the colonial habit of reading Maroon spiritual authority as obeah.

figures.gloss: A public healing rite in which the specialist retrieves a sick person's lost 'shadow' — the vital double, believed stolen away or nailed to a cotton tree by malicious agency — and restores it to the afflicted patient.
figures.facet: It treats illness as a spatial problem before a medical one: the patient is not broken but incomplete, and the cure is retrieval rather than remedy — the specialist as tracker of a missing part of the person rather than healer of the whole.
figures.provenance: Martha Warren Beckwith, 'Black Roadways: A Study of Jamaican Folk Life' (1929).

figures.gloss: The great ceiba tree, held across the Anglo-Caribbean to be the dwelling place of duppies and the site where shadows are nailed and grave-side work is done; felling one is hedged with precautions in folklore throughout the region.
figures.facet: It is obeah's fixed geography in an otherwise portable craft: while the practitioner, the bundle, and the client relationship travel anywhere, the silk-cotton tree is the one place the dead are known to actually live, giving the region's ghost-traffic a literal address.
figures.provenance: Martha Warren Beckwith, 'Black Roadways: A Study of Jamaican Folk Life' (1929); Diana Paton, 'The Cultural Politics of Obeah' (2015).

figures.gloss: Editions from the Chicago mail-order occult publisher L. W. de Laurence — including his versions of the 'Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses' — absorbed into 20th-century Caribbean practice as obeah's working library; the catalogue was consequential enough that Jamaica banned its importation outright.
figures.facet: It marks the craft's transition from orally transmitted, herb-and-grave-dirt technique to book-sourced power: 'de Laurence' became shorthand across the region for power obtained from the printed page rather than inherited apprenticeship, and the colonial and postcolonial state answered it, tellingly, with a customs ban rather than a folklore rebuttal.
figures.provenance: Diana Paton, 'The Cultural Politics of Obeah' (2015), which treats the Jamaican de Laurence import ban at length; Jamaica's Obeah Act amendments restricting de Laurence publications.

edges.note: The same craft: the Jamaican shard names obeah as a force within its island complex; this shard treats the pan-Anglo-Caribbean practice in its own right.
The duppy is the very spirit obeah catches and sets — one spirit ecology shared across the Anglo-Caribbean and its Jamaican shard.
Myal historically organized itself as the communal, congregational antidote to the obeah-man's private, hired craft — the attested polarity of Jamaican spiritual work.
Parallel descendants of the same African bundle-technology in the Anglo-Caribbean and the US South — cognate craft, not descent of one from the other.
The obeah bundle carries forward the Kongo nkisi principle of binding spirit-force into a constructed, fed container of grave dirt and organic matter.
Both crafts contract the restless dead as spiritual mercenaries through grave materials — kindred Afro-Atlantic techniques on either side of the Caribbean.

The rebellion oath's grave-dirt-and-blood draught invokes the binding witness of the dead in the manner of Gold Coast (Akan) oath practice carried by Coromantee captives (Vincent Brown 2020); the further claim that the word 'obeah' itself is Akan (obayifo) is contested — tier-flagged, not asserted.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: the same professional office of paid conjure specialist, combining divination, healing, and hexing, in the US South and the Anglo-Caribbean — parallel descendants of shared African roots, not one derived from the other.
Intra-tradition: Jack's protective charm is the archetypal client-transaction the practitioner entry describes, made legend — the specialist's hired craft turned into an outlaw's personal armor.
Intra-tradition: shadow-catching and duppy catching/setting are the paired core techniques of the same specialist's repertoire, both documented in Beckwith (1929).
The same divisible-soul concept and the same retrieval technique named on both sides of the pan-Anglo-Caribbean / Jamaican-specific split in this corpus.
Myal's central rite is exactly this technique run congregationally rather than by a single hired specialist — same craft, different register.
The Jamaican shard's own duppy entry names the cottonwood tree as the ghost's haunt — the same tree, the same spirit ecology, described from the two shards' respective angles.
Intra-tradition: de Laurence's catalogue became part of the specialist's working toolkit alongside herbs and grave dirt, per Paton (2015).

Two Afro-Atlantic crafts each absorbing a mail-order or print grimoire tradition (de Laurence's catalogue in the Caribbean, biblical psalm-magic manuals in the US South) into an oral practice — parallel print-sourced power, not shared descent.
obia and obeah are cognate terms for charged magical craft and substance, both rooted in West African vocabulary carried into separate Caribbean and Guianese diasporas.

dossier.essence: Obeah is the individual craft of spiritual healing, protection, divination, and harm practiced across the Anglophone Caribbean — Jamaica, Barbados, Trinidad, Guyana, and the smaller islands — by hired specialists rather than congregations. Forged by enslaved West and West-Central Africans in the eighteenth century, it is a client-based spiritual technology more than an organized religion, and it has been a crime for most of its documented existence: Jamaica's first Obeah Act followed Tacky's Revolt in 1760, and successor statutes remain on the books in several jurisdictions.
dossier.cosmos: Obeah carries no unified creation story or doctrine of salvation; it presupposes a world in which spiritual force is real, morally neutral, and transferable into worked materials — grave dirt, blood, bones, pins — and in which the dead remain active parties. The restless ghost (in Jamaica, the duppy) can be caught, bottled, set upon an enemy, or pulled off an afflicted victim, making the dead a workable resource rather than objects of worship.
dossier.practice: The unit of practice is the private consultation: a client pays the obeah-man or obeah-woman for healing, protection, court-case luck, love, revenge, or the undoing of another practitioner's work, executed through baths, powders, and constructed bundles buried, hung, or carried. There is no church, communal liturgy, or priesthood; historically the specialist also administered binding oaths to slave conspiracies, which is what drew capital legislation in 1760. Criminalization pushed the craft under a legal shadow it has never fully left.
dossier.canon: No scripture: an oral craft transmitted practitioner to practitioner; the largest written archive is, ironically, hostile — colonial statutes, trial records, and planter histories such as Edward Long's 'The History of Jamaica' (1774).
dossier.reflects: Religion contracted rather than congregated — the impulse to secure spiritual power through a private, paid transaction with a specialist instead of through collective worship.
sources: Diana Paton, 'The Cultural Politics of Obeah' (2015)
Jerome S. Handler & Kenneth M. Bilby, 'Enacting Power: The Criminalization of Obeah in the Anglophone Caribbean, 1760-2011' (2012)
Vincent Brown, 'Tacky's Revolt: The Story of an Atlantic Slave War' (2020)

links.cite: Diana Paton, 'The Cultural Politics of Obeah' (2015)
